channel.title: math on mahmood
channel.description: Recent content in math on mahmood

item.title: l2 loss
item.description: with a predictive hypothesis and a target hypothesis , the least squares loss function is defined by where such that the latter is the set of observations from the target hypothesis . consider the linear model where is a parameter space, are input features and are the corresponding observations. we define the functions we seek , and we will use the chain rule for this purpose, is the least-squares loss function.

item.title: exporting from org mode with pandoc
item.description: motivation the need for an emacs-independent exporting of notes arose from the fact that i was using org version 9.7-pre in which exporting functionality is basically broken. pandoc because i didnt want to reinvent the wheel, i landed on using pandoc, it has a huge community and i trust it to maintain org-mode parsability in the long run. there are some missing features in pandoc exporting which is why im writing this.

item.title: deterministic finite automaton
item.description: a finite automaton is a 5-tuple , where is a finite set called the states, is a finite set called the alphabet, is the transition function, is the start state, and is the set of accept states. (Michael Sipser, 2012 definition 1.5) a configuration of a finite automaton is an element of . the current configuration of an automaton is a pair (Mark K. Goldberg, ????) given a DFA , for configurations such that , we write iff .

item.title: regular expression
item.description: regular expressions differ in syntax from one context to another, and the theory behind them is vaster than i had thought, they are usually studied in automata theory. the operators of regular expressions make use of the following 3 operations on languages: union of languages concatenation of languages closure of a language we call them the regular operations, regular languages are closed under each one of them. (John E. Hopcroft, Rajeev Motwani, Jeffrey D.

item.title: conv gan with pytorch
item.description: some information was quoted from this great article (the code was rewritten in pytorch and alot of things differ though!). essential packages we need to import some essential packages: # <<src-activate-venv()>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F torch.manual_seed(0) # for reproducibility it’s common PyTorch practice to initialize a variable, usually named device, that will hold the device we’re training on (CPU or GPU).

item.title: symbolic logic in common lisp
item.description: logic programming in common lisp logical statements we make use of the symbolic capabilities of lisp, we use quoting and symbols to represent logical statements, e.g. given the following knowledge base of horn clauses: in lisp we could write it as: (defparameter *kb* '((implies p q) (implies (and l m) p) (implies (and b l) m) (implies (and a p) l) (implies (and a b s) l) A B)) *kb* ((IMPLIES P Q) (IMPLIES (AND L M) P) (IMPLIES (AND B L) M) (IMPLIES (AND A P) L) (IMPLIES (AND A B S) L) A B) the choice of the symbols implies, and could vary, but essentially we should write code that deals properly with these symbols based on their names.

item.title: logic formulas
item.description: note that and represent true and false, respectively propositional logic equivalence number equivalence rule name E1 E2 E3 E4 E5 E6 E7 E8 E9 E10 E11 E12 E13 E14 distributivity E15 distributivity E16 de morgan E17 de morgan E18 E19 E20 E21 E22 E23 logical implication number implication rule name I1 I2 I3 I4 I5 I6 I7 I8 I9 I10 I11 I12 I13 I14 first-order logic logical implication number implication I'1 I'2 I'3 I'4 I'5 I'6 I'7 I'8 I'9 I'10 rule of inference name rule of inference Universal Instantiation where is some arbitrary element of the universe Universal Generalization where holds for every element of the universe Existential Instantiation where is some element of the universe of discourse.

item.title: convolutional neural network
item.description: citations (Peter Norvig, Stuart J. Russell, 2020) (Jianxin Wu, 2017) (Zhifei Zhang, 2016) and countless other resources that helped me form a good understanding of the topics related to conv networks motivation an image cannot be thought of as a simple vector of input pixel values, primarily because adjacency of pixels in an image matters. simple multilayer perceptrons arent suited for images because adjacent neurons in the same layer arent really connected or affected by each other, which isnt the case in images.

item.title: maxima in lisp
item.description: i ended up giving up on using maxima as a visualization/symbolic math computation library for lisp, it wasnt made with this usecase in mind i guess, and so its an utter nightmare to lisp with it maxima is written in lisp and so it can be used as a modified/extended lisp runtime how to to start the lisp runtime use the command: rmaxima -r 'to_lisp();' can also run this in emacs’ slime/sly with (sly "rmaxima -r to_lisp();") alternatively, we can clone maxima’s source code to the subdirectory local-projects of the quicklisp installation directory (usually ~/quicklisp unless you’ve modified it), follow the instructions at https://sourceforge.

item.title: feedforward neural network
item.description: feedforward neural networks is a wider term than discussed here, whats discussed here really is a specific type we call a simple multilayer perceptron, but this serves as an introductory to other types of more complex networks a feedforward network has connections only in one direction. each node computes a function of its inputs and passes the result to its successors in the network. information flows through the network from the input nodes to the output nodes, and there are no loops.
